narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, including a commendable LSAT score of 40 that stands out above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 67% chance that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger projected odds of bar success. The key factors driving this prediction were your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, which falls below the average of 3.21 and had the strongest pull toward forecasting failure, suggesting it signaled potential challenges in sustaining high performance through law school and beyond. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3, lower than the typical 5.71, was the next biggest influence pushing in the same concerning direction, indicating early academic struggles relative to peers. While your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, countering the risk by highlighting your aptitude for the rigors of legal reasoning, it wasn't quite enough to offset those academic red flags. Other elements, like your Year 3 decile of 4 (still below average) and family income bracket, played smaller roles in tipping the scales toward failure, while your full-time status aligned with the norm and had minimal impact. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your profile shows strengths in test-taking and socioeconomic support but lags in core academic metrics that the model weighs heavily for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's just how your numbers stacked up against patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your GPA and decile rankings, as even modest improvements there could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also seek targeted LSAT-style prep to build on your already strong score, or explore schools with more holistic reviews. We're rooting for you—many applicants turn rejections into comebacks by addressing these exact areas. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
